expanded_description: Learn to calculate the percent composition by mass of elements in compounds. Master the relationship between chemical formulas, molar mass, and elemental mass percentages used in analytical chemistry and formula determination.

core_concept.principle: Percent Composition Reveals Elemental Mass Ratios
core_concept.explanation: Percent composition expresses the mass percentage of each element in a compound. It is calculated by dividing the total mass of an element in one mole of compound by the molar mass of the compound, then multiplying by 100%. This fundamental calculation connects chemical formulas to experimental mass data and enables determination of empirical formulas from analytical results.

image.alt: Diagram showing mass breakdown of elements in a compound
image.caption: Figure 8.5.1: Percent composition shows what fraction of a compound's mass comes from each element.

sections.introduction: Percent composition by mass is a fundamental analytical tool in chemistry. It tells us what percentage of a compound's total mass is contributed by each element, enabling comparison of different compounds and verification of chemical formulas.

key_concept.definition: Percent composition by mass is the mass percentage of each element in a compound, calculated as: $\text{\% Element} = \dfrac{\text{mass of element in 1 mol compound}}{\text{molar mass of compound}} \times 100\%$.
key_concept.context: Percent composition is independent of sample size: a 1 g sample and a 100 g sample of the same compound have identical percent compositions.

components.equation: $\text{\% Element} = \dfrac{(\text{number of atoms}) \times (\text{atomic mass})}{\text{molar mass of compound}} \times 100\%$
components.interpretation: The numerator is the total mass contribution of the element in one mole of compound; the denominator is the total mass of one mole of compound.

image.alt: Formula diagram for percent composition calculation
image.caption: Figure 8.5.2: The percent composition formula relates atomic masses to compound molar mass.

characteristics: Uses atomic masses from periodic table
classifications.behavior: This calculation predicts what percent composition should be for a pure compound with the given formula.

image.caption: Water is 88.81% oxygen by mass despite having more H atoms.
Oxygen dominates CO2 mass despite 1:2 atom ratio.

characteristics: Mass of each element measured separately
classifications.behavior: Experimental percent composition enables determination of empirical formulas when the compound identity is unknown.

examples.explanation: Burn sample, collect $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, calculate masses of C and H, determine $\text{\% C}$ and $\text{\% H}$ by mass.
Heat metal to form oxide, measure mass gain from oxygen, calculate $\text{\% metal}$ and $\text{\% O}$ in the oxide.

classifications.value: Practical Uses in Chemistry and Industry
classifications.behavior: Percent composition data guides decisions in research, manufacturing, and quality control.

examples.explanation: Fertilizer labels show $\text{\% N}$, $\text{\% P}_2\text{O}_5$, $\text{\% K}_2\text{O}$ by mass; farmers use these to match crop needs.
Hematite ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$) is $69.94\%$ Fe by mass; higher $\text{\% Fe}$ means more valuable ore.

principles.principle: Percent Composition Is Independent of Sample Size
principles.statement: The mass percentage of each element in a pure compound is constant regardless of sample amount, reflecting the law of definite proportions.
principles.application: Whether you have $1.00~\text{g}$ or $100.0~\text{g}$ of $\text{NaCl}$, it is always $39.34\%$ Na and $60.66\%$ Cl by mass.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe ratio: a cake is always 30% flour by mass whether you make one serving or one hundred.

principles.statement: The mass percentages of all elements in a compound must sum to 100% (within rounding error), providing a check on calculations.
principles.application: For $\text{H}_2\text{O}$: $11.19\%~\text{H} + 88.81\%~\text{O} = 100.00\%$. If your sum differs significantly, recalculate.
principles.example: This principle helps catch arithmetic errors in percent composition calculations.

sections.introduction: When the chemical formula is known, percent composition is calculated systematically using atomic masses and the molar mass of the compound.

key_concept.definition: The step-by-step method: (1) Calculate molar mass of compound, (2) For each element, multiply atom count by atomic mass, (3) Divide element mass by compound molar mass, (4) Multiply by 100%.
key_concept.context: This method works for any compound with a known formula, from simple binaries to complex organic molecules.

process_flow.description: Ensure the formula is correct with proper subscripts and parentheses. Verify the compound name matches the formula.
Sum the atomic masses of all atoms in the formula, accounting for subscripts and parentheses.

process_flow.process: Calculate Mass Contribution of Each Element
process_flow.description: For each element: (number of atoms) × (atomic mass) = mass contribution per mole of compound.
Divide each element's mass contribution by the compound's molar mass, then multiply by 100%.
Add all percent values; they should sum to 100% (allowing for rounding error).

worked_examples.title: Simple Binary Compound: Water
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the percent composition by mass of hydrogen and oxygen in water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$).
worked_examples.final_answer: Water is $11.19\%$ hydrogen and $88.81\%$ oxygen by mass. Note that despite having twice as many H atoms as O atoms, oxygen dominates the mass because O atoms are much heavier.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Percent Composition Calculation for Binary Compound

worked_examples.title: Compound with Polyatomic Ion: Calcium Nitrate
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the percent composition of calcium, nitrogen, and oxygen in calcium nitrate, $\text{Ca}(\text{NO}_3)_2$.
worked_examples.final_answer: Calcium nitrate is $24.42\%$ Ca, $17.07\%$ N, and $58.50\%$ O by mass. Careful atom counting in the polyatomic ion ($\text{NO}_3^-$) is essential for accurate results.

worked_examples.title: Hydrate Compound: Copper(II) Sulfate Pentahydrate
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the percent composition of water in copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

step_by_step.step: Calculate Molar Mass of Anhydrous Part

worked_examples.final_answer: Copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate is $36.08\%$ water by mass. When heated, this water can be driven off, leaving anhydrous $\text{CuSO}_4$. The significant water content explains why hydrates often have lower metal percentages than their anhydrous forms.

worked_examples.title: Organic Compound: Glucose
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the percent composition of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen in glucose, $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$.
worked_examples.final_answer: Glucose is $40.00\%$ C, $6.714\%$ H, and $53.29\%$ O by mass. These values are used in biochemical calculations and nutritional labeling. Note that oxygen contributes the most mass despite equal numbers of C and O atoms.

sections.introduction: Percent composition data enables various practical calculations: finding element mass in a sample, comparing compounds, and determining empirical formulas from experimental data.

key_concept.definition: Percent composition serves as a conversion factor: $\text{mass of element} = \text{mass of compound} \times \dfrac{\text{\% element}}{100\%}$. This enables mass-to-mass conversions within compounds.
key_concept.context: These calculations are essential in analytical chemistry, industrial processing, and environmental monitoring.

categories.type: Finding Mass of Element in Sample
categories.description: Given the mass of a compound and its percent composition, calculate the mass of a specific element contained in the sample.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Use percent composition as a conversion factor: multiply sample mass by (percent element / 100) to get element mass.

examples.explanation: Hematite is $69.94\%$ Fe. In $50.0~\text{g}$ of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$: $50.0~\text{g} \times 0.6994 = 35.0~\text{g}$ Fe.

image.caption: Percent composition finds element mass in sample.

examples.explanation: Ammonium nitrate ($\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$) is $35.00\%$ N. A $10.0~\text{kg}$ bag contains $10.0~\text{kg} \times 0.3500 = 3.50~\text{kg}$ N.

image.caption: Percent N guides fertilizer application rates.

categories.type: Comparing Element Content in Different Compounds
categories.description: Use percent composition to determine which compound contains more of a desired element per unit mass.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Calculate or look up percent composition for each compound; higher percentage means more element per gram of compound.

examples.explanation: $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$: $69.94\%$ Fe; $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4$: $72.36\%$ Fe. Magnetite ($\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4$) has higher iron content.

examples.process: Which fertilizer has more N: $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ or $\text{(NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$?
examples.explanation: $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$: $35.00\%$ N; $\text{(NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$: $21.20\%$ N. Ammonium nitrate is more nitrogen-rich.

categories.type: Determining Empirical Formula from Percent Composition
categories.description: Experimental percent composition data enables determination of the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Assume 100 g sample (so percents become grams), convert grams to moles for each element, divide by smallest mole value to get ratios, multiply to get whole numbers.

examples.explanation: Assume 100 g: C: $40.0~\text{g} \div 12.01 = 3.33~\text{mol}$; H: $6.71~\text{g} \div 1.008 = 6.66~\text{mol}$; O: $53.3~\text{g} \div 16.00 = 3.33~\text{mol}$. Ratio: $1:2:1$ → $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$.

principles.statement: Percent composition provides a direct mass-to-mass conversion within a compound: $\text{mass}_{\text{element}} = \text{mass}_{\text{compound}} \times \dfrac{\text{\% element}}{100}$.
principles.application: To find mass of O in $25.0~\text{g}$ of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$: $25.0~\text{g} \times 0.8881 = 22.2~\text{g}$ O.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe: if a cake is 30% flour by mass, a 500 g cake contains $500 \times 0.30 = 150$ g flour.

principles.principle: Assume 100 g for Empirical Formula Calculations
principles.statement: When determining empirical formulas from percent composition, assuming a 100 g sample converts percentages directly to grams, simplifying calculations.
principles.application: $40.0\%$ C becomes $40.0~\text{g}$ C in a 100 g sample; no additional conversion needed.
principles.example: This assumption is valid because percent composition is independent of sample size.

sections.introduction: Apply percent composition concepts to realistic chemistry problems. These examples demonstrate the types of calculations you'll encounter in analytical and industrial chemistry.

worked_examples.title: Mass of Element in Compound Sample
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of aluminum are in $125~\text{g}$ of aluminum oxide, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$?

step_by_step.step: Calculate Mass of Al in Sample

worked_examples.final_answer: $66.2~\text{g}$ of aluminum are in $125~\text{g}$ of $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$. This calculation is essential for determining metal yield from ore processing.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Element Mass from Compound Mass Using Percent Composition

worked_examples.title: Comparing Nitrogen Content in Fertilizers
worked_examples.problem: Which fertilizer provides more nitrogen per kilogram: urea ($\text{CO}(\text{NH}_2)_2$) or ammonium sulfate ($\text{(NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$)? Calculate the mass of N in $1.00~\text{kg}$ of each.

step_by_step.step: Calculate Mass of N in 1 kg Urea
Calculate Percent N in Ammonium Sulfate
Calculate Mass of N in 1 kg Ammonium Sulfate

worked_examples.final_answer: Urea provides more nitrogen per kilogram: $467~\text{g}$ N vs. $212~\text{g}$ N in ammonium sulfate. Urea's higher nitrogen content ($46.65\%$ vs. $21.20\%$) makes it more efficient for nitrogen delivery, though other factors (cost, solubility, soil pH effects) also influence fertilizer choice.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comparing Element Content in Different Compounds

worked_examples.title: Empirical Formula from Percent Composition
worked_examples.problem: A compound is analyzed and found to contain $52.14\%$ C, $13.13\%$ H, and $34.73\%$ O by mass. Determine the empirical formula.

step_by_step.explanation: Percents become grams: $52.14~\text{g}$ C, $13.13~\text{g}$ H, $34.73~\text{g}$ O.

worked_examples.final_answer: The empirical formula is $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$. This could represent ethanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) or dimethyl ether ($\text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3$); additional data (molar mass, spectral analysis) would distinguish these isomers.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Empirical Formula Determination from Percent Composition

worked_examples.title: Hydrate Water Content Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A $2.50~\text{g}$ sample of hydrated copper(II) sulfate, $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot x\text{H}_2\text{O}$, is heated to remove water, leaving $1.60~\text{g}$ of anhydrous $\text{CuSO}_4$. Calculate the percent water in the hydrate and determine the value of $x$.

step_by_step.explanation: Mass water = initial mass - final mass = $2.50~\text{g} - 1.60~\text{g} = 0.90~\text{g}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: The hydrate is $36.0\%$ water by mass, and the formula is $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate). This experimental method determines hydrate formulas by measuring mass loss upon heating.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hydrate Formula Determination from Mass Data

worked_examples.title: Percent Composition Verification
worked_examples.problem: A student calculates the percent composition of $\text{Mg}(\text{NO}_3)_2$ as $16.39\%$ Mg, $18.89\%$ N, and $64.72\%$ O. Verify these values and identify any errors.
worked_examples.final_answer: The student's calculations are correct: $\text{Mg}(\text{NO}_3)_2$ is $16.39\%$ Mg, $18.89\%$ N, and $64.72\%$ O by mass. The sum equals 100.00%, confirming internal consistency. This verification process is essential for catching calculation errors in analytical work.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Percent Composition Verification and Error Checking

sections.introduction: Avoid these frequent errors when calculating and using percent composition; apply expert strategies for accurate analytical work.

key_concept.definition: Common percent composition errors include miscounting atoms in formulas, using atomic number instead of atomic mass, forgetting to multiply by 100%, and mishandling significant figures.
key_concept.context: Careful attention to formulas, units, and calculation steps prevents errors that propagate through empirical formula determinations and analytical results.

characteristics: Forgetting to multiply by subscript outside parentheses
Not counting all atoms in hydrates
classifications.behavior: Incorrect atom counts lead to wrong molar masses and incorrect percent compositions, which then cause errors in all subsequent calculations.

examples.explanation: Correct: subscript 2 applies to entire $\text{NO}_3$ group: 2 N and 6 O. Molar mass error: $108.01$ vs. $164.10~\text{g/mol}$.

image.caption: Parentheses subscript multiplies all atoms inside.

classifications.type: Mistake: Using Atomic Number Instead of Atomic Mass
characteristics: Confusing periodic table position with mass
classifications.behavior: Atomic number counts protons; atomic mass (weighted average of isotopes) is needed for mass calculations. Using the wrong value produces nonsensical results.

examples.process: Wrong: C atomic number = 6 for mass calculation
examples.explanation: Correct: C atomic mass = 12.01 for mass calculations. Using 6 gives percent values half of correct values.

image.caption: Use atomic mass (bottom number) for calculations.

characteristics: Reporting 0.1119 instead of 11.19% for H in water
Confusing mass fraction with mass percent
Sum of values equals 1.00 instead of 100%
classifications.behavior: Percent means 'per hundred'; forgetting the ×100% step gives mass fractions, not percents.

image.caption: Multiply by 100% to convert fraction to percent.

characteristics: Reporting too many digits from calculator
Not matching sig figs to given atomic masses
Sum of rounded percents not equaling 100%
classifications.behavior: Significant figures reflect measurement precision; incorrect sig figs misrepresent the certainty of analytical results.

examples.process: Given atomic masses to 4 sig figs
examples.explanation: Final percents should have 4 sig figs: 11.19% H, not 11.187654%. Round only the final answer, not intermediate values.

image.caption: Match sig figs to precision of input data.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Use the 100 g Assumption for Empirical Formulas
characteristics: Percents become grams directly in 100 g sample
classifications.behavior: This assumption is valid because percent composition is independent of sample size; it simplifies empirical formula determinations.

examples.explanation: Assume 100 g: 40.0 g C, 6.71 g H, 53.3 g O. Convert directly to moles without extra factors.

image.caption: 100 g assumption simplifies empirical formula work.

characteristics: Add all element percents after calculation
classifications.behavior: This verification step catches atom counting errors, molar mass mistakes, and arithmetic errors before they propagate.

examples.explanation: 11.19% H + 88.81% O = 100.00%. If sum is 99.5% or 101.2%, recalculate to find the error.

principles.principle: Percent Composition Is a Fundamental Analytical Tool
principles.statement: Percent composition connects chemical formulas to experimental mass data, enabling empirical formula determination, purity assessment, and quantitative analysis.
principles.application: From fertilizer labels to ore valuation to pharmaceutical quality control, percent composition data guides critical decisions in chemistry and industry.
principles.analogy: Like a nutritional label: percent composition tells you 'what's in' a compound, just as food labels tell you what's in your meal.

principles.statement: Always verify that percent compositions sum to 100% and that calculated element masses match the original sample mass. These checks catch most calculation errors.
principles.application: After calculating percents for $\text{Ca}(\text{NO}_3)_2$, verify: $24.42\% + 17.07\% + 58.50\% = 99.99\%$ (acceptable rounding). If sum is 95%, find the error.
principles.example: This habit of verification is essential for reliable analytical chemistry work.

summary: Percent composition by mass expresses the mass percentage of each element in a compound: $\text{\% Element} = \dfrac{\text{mass of element in 1 mol}}{\text{molar mass of compound}} \times 100\%$. Calculation steps: (1) determine molar mass from formula, (2) calculate each element's mass contribution, (3) divide by molar mass and multiply by 100%. Percent composition is independent of sample size and must sum to 100% (within rounding). Applications include: finding element mass in samples ($\text{mass}_{\text{element}} = \text{mass}_{\text{compound}} \times \text{\%}/100$), comparing compounds for desired element content, and determining empirical formulas from experimental data (assume 100 g sample, convert percents to grams, then to moles, find simplest ratio). Common mistakes include miscounting atoms (especially with parentheses), using atomic number instead of atomic mass, forgetting the ×100% factor, and significant figure errors. Critical skills: careful atom counting in formulas, proper use of atomic masses, dimensional analysis with percent as conversion factor, and verification that percents sum to 100%. Mastery of percent composition is foundational for empirical formula determination, analytical chemistry, and quantitative analysis in research and industry.
